reasonForServices: Amber presented seeking help for her substance use, stating: "I need help with my substance use, alcohol and meth."
currentBehavioralSymptoms: Amber presents with chronic anxiety (9-10/10 severity) with racing thoughts, restlessness, and poor concentration. She reports depressive symptoms including low mood,  decreased motivation, crying spells, irritability, mood lability, feelings of worthlessness, and low energy. She has significant sleep disturbance, not falling asleep until 2-3 AM. Behaviorally, she demonstrates impulsivity, fear of abandonment, and emotional reactivity in relationships. treatmentObjectives:
Alcohol Use Disorder, Severe
* Ms. Amber will stay sober from alcohol and work to stay sober longer than her previous longest time of 2 months.
* Ms. Amber will stop having blackout episodes by staying completely sober and taking Naltrexone 50mg every day as prescribed.
* Ms. Amber will identify specific triggers related to her reservation environment and family/community substance use patterns, and learn healthier ways to respond to each trigger.
* Ms. Amber will talk about how starting to drink at age 13 and growing up in a community where drinking was normal has affected her current alcohol use.
* Ms. Amber will explain how alcohol has interfered with her goals of staying sober and keeping stable employment.
Stimulant Use Disorder (Methamphetamine)
* Ms. Amber will continue staying sober from methamphetamine and build on the 2+ months of sobriety she had when she entered treatment.
* Ms. Amber will understand how meth use has been connected to her anxiety, sleep problems, and mood changes.
* Ms. Amber will learn to recognize early warning signs of cravings and use coping skills before a relapse happens.
Generalized Anxiety Disorder
* Ms. Amber will lower her anxiety from severe levels (9–10 out of 10) to 5 out of 10 or less by using Hydroxyzine as needed and practicing coping skills.
* Ms. Amber will manage racing thoughts by practicing CBT thought-stopping and grounding techniques every day.
* Ms. Amber will improve her focus and ability to participate in treatment without anxiety getting in the way.
* Ms. Amber will learn the difference between anxiety symptoms and possible side effects from Bupropion, and report any worsening symptoms to the treatment team.
Major Depressive Disorder (Provisional)
* Ms. Amber will improve her mood within 4–6 weeks of starting Bupropion XL 150mg.
* Ms. Amber will reduce crying episodes so they happen rarely instead of often.
* Ms. Amber will increase her energy and motivation by actively participating in daily treatment and self-care activities.
* Ms. Amber will challenge feelings of worthlessness by identifying her strengths and past successes, including staying sober for 2 months and taking the step to seek treatment.
* Ms. Amber will monitor how she responds to Bupropion and report any side effects such as trouble sleeping, increased anxiety, or feeling overly activated.
Coping Skills Development
* Ms. Amber will develop at least five healthy coping strategies that she can use on her own.
* Ms. Amber will work on changing unhealthy coping habits that developed from early substance use and family challenges.
* Ms. Amber will build stress tolerance by gradually facing stress in a safe residential setting.
Family & Relationship Patterns
* Ms. Amber will process how growing up with parents who had depression and anxiety has affected her mental health.
* Ms. Amber will explore how substance use was normalized in her family and reservation community, without judgment.
* Ms. Amber will learn to recognize healthy and unhealthy relationship patterns, especially related to her fear of abandonment.
* Ms. Amber will strengthen her relationships with her siblings, nieces, and nephews as part of her sober support system.
dischargePlanObjectives:
Housing & Living Environment
* Ms. Amber will move into a licensed sober living or transitional home before discharge and avoid returning to environments where substance use is common.
* She will choose housing with structure, such as curfews and drug testing, to support her recovery.
* She will confirm the housing is stable and properly licensed.
* If she must return to the reservation, she will create a clear safety plan to manage triggers and high-risk situations.
Step-Down Level of Care
* Ms. Amber will step down to a lower level of care, such as residential treatment (ASAM 3.1) or Intensive Outpatient (IOP).
* The program will treat both her substance use and mental health needs.
* Her records and treatment summary will be shared with the new provider.
Medication Continuity
* Ms. Amber will leave with a 30-day supply of medications or confirmed pharmacy pickup.
* She will receive a written list of her medications and understand their purpose.
* She will continue taking Naltrexone as prescribed to manage alcohol cravings.
Recovery Support Network
* Ms. Amber will identify at least three sober supports she can contact during cravings.
* She will attend AA, NA, Wellbriety, or tribal recovery meetings.
* She will try to find a sponsor and stay connected to supportive peers.
Employment & Vocational Planning
* Ms. Amber will connect with vocational or tribal employment services.
* She will update her resume and practice interview skills.
* She will begin applying for jobs within two weeks of discharge and consider part-time work to support early recovery.

- Co-occurring anxiety, depression, and mood instability

- Needs 24-hour structured support, medication monitoring, and intensive therapeutic intervention
Treatment Components:
- Structured residential programming
- Relapse prevention therapy
- CBT for impulse control and anxiety management
- Medication-assisted treatment (Naltrexone)
- Random urine drug screens

crisisInterventionPlan:
Warning Signs
- Increased anxiety, irritability, or mood swings
- Worsening sleep or staying up later than usual
- Isolation from peers or avoiding treatment activities
- Crying spells, hopelessness, or feelings of worthlessness
- Talking about or romanticizing past substance use
- Contacting people who use substances
- Intense cravings or actively seeking substances
- Suicidal thoughts or statements
- Signs of hypomanic episode (decreased sleep need, elevated mood, impulsivity)

Triggers
- Reservation environment or contact with family/community members who use substances
- Emotional distress, perceived rejection, or fear of abandonment
- Interpersonal conflict
- Sleep problems or insomnia
- Boredom, unstructured time, or isolation
- Medication non-compliance

Coping Strategies
- Grounding: 5-4-3-2-1 sensory technique, deep breathing (4-7-8)
- TIPP skills: Temperature (cold water/ice), Intense exercise, Paced breathing, Paired muscle relaxation
- Distraction: Walk, music, journaling, physical activity, attend extra meeting
- Cognitive: "Play the tape forward," challenge negative thoughts, review reasons for sobriety
- Reach out: Talk to staff, peer, or sponsor; attend AA/NA/Wellbriety meeting
- PRN medication: Hydroxyzine 25mg for anxiety, Trazodone 50mg for sleep

Restrictions
- Avoid reservation areas with prevalent substance use
- No contact with dealers or actively using friends/family
- Avoid bars, liquor stores, and high-risk locations
- Avoid prolonged isolation
- Medications managed by staff while in BHRF

Crisis Resources
- 988 – Suicide & Crisis Lifeline (call/text)
- 1-800-631-1314 – Arizona Crisis Response Network
- 1-800-327-4880 – Solari Crisis Line (AHCCCS)
- 911 – Life-threatening emergencies
- Psychiatrist: Francis Kahiga, PMHNP-BC – (214) 538-3571
- Sponsor/Sober Supports: To be identified during treatment

Diagnoses
- F10.20 – Alcohol Use Disorder, Severe
- F15.10 – Stimulant Use Disorder (Methamphetamine), Mild to Moderate
- F41.1 – Generalized Anxiety Disorder
- F33.1 – Major Depressive Disorder, Recurrent, Moderate (provisional)
- F31.81 – Rule Out Bipolar II Disorder (provisional)
- F51.01 – Insomnia Disorder
dischargePlanning:
Key Considerations
- High relapse risk due to limited sustained sobriety (max 2 months), early-onset use, and high-risk home environment
- Reservation environment has prevalent substance use among family and community; returning without support increases relapse risk
- Previous treatment ended due to facility closure, not her relapse – she may succeed with stable, licensed placement
- Limited support system – no spouse/children, family has mental health issues and substance use; sober network needs to be built

Discharge Goals

Housing:

- Avoid return to reservation without strong recovery supports in place

Treatment Continuity:

Recovery Support:

Functional Goals:

Safety:
- Complete written relapse prevention plan with identified triggers and coping strategies
- Complete crisis/safety plan with emergency contacts
- Verbalize commitment to aftercare and ability to articulate discharge plan

Discharge Criteria
- Maintained sobriety throughout BHRF stay
- Mood and anxiety stabilized on current medications
- No hypomanic/bipolar symptoms requiring higher level of care
- Safe, sober housing confirmed
- Outpatient appointments scheduled
- Relapse prevention and crisis plans completed
- Demonstrates use of coping skills independently

treatmentObjectives:
1. Client will maintain abstinence from fentanyl, methamphetamine, and alcohol as evidenced by negative drug screens throughout residential treatment.
2. Client will engage in medication-assisted treatment (MAT) by taking Suboxone as prescribed and attending all medication management appointments.
3. Client will identify at least 5 personal relapse triggers (e.g., stress, habit, emotional dysregulation) and develop 2-3 coping strategies for each trigger within 30 days.
4. Client will complete residential substance use treatment programming and attend at least 90% of scheduled individual and group therapy sessions.
5. Client will report a reduction in anxiety symptoms from 10/10 to 6/10 or below within 30 days through medication management and coping skill development.
6. Client will report a reduction in depression symptoms from 7/10 to 4/10 or below within 30 days as measured by self-report and clinical observation.
7. Client will work with case management to secure stable housing
8. Client will learn and demonstrate at least 3 healthy coping skills (e.g., grounding techniques, deep breathing, distress tolerance) to manage cravings and emotional distress.

reasonsForReferral: Mr. Dwayne is being referred for placement in a Behavioral Health Residential Facility (BHRF) because he needs help with his alcohol addiction and related mental health issues. He has been drinking heavily since he was 17 years old, and his drinking got worse when he joined the military. He has been trying to stay sober on his own but has not been able to maintain it. He also has PTSD, depression, anxiety, and trouble sleeping that need treatment. Because he is homeless, unemployed, and has no support system, he needs a structured place where he can get the help he needs to recover.
residentNeeds:
Mr. Dwayne needs:
- A safe, structured place to live while he works on his recovery
- Treatment for his severe alcohol use disorder
- Help with his PTSD, depression, anxiety, and sleep problems
- Help finding housing after treatment
- Help connecting with VA (Veterans Affairs) services
- Support building a sober network of people
- Help managing daily cravings for alcohol
- Case management to help with housing and other needs

treatmentRecommendation:
Recommended Level of Care:
ASAM Level 3.1 - Clinically Managed Low-Intensity Residential Services (Behavioral Health Residential Facility)

Reason for This Level of Care:
- Mr. Dwayne has a long history of severe alcohol use disorder spanning over 40 years
- He has been unable to maintain sobriety on his own (longest sobriety was 6 weeks)
- He has co-occurring mental health conditions (PTSD, depression, anxiety, insomnia)
- He is homeless and has no stable support system
- He has high relapse risk due to cravings, untreated trauma, and unstable environment
- He needs a structured therapeutic environment to stabilize

Recommended Services:

Substance Use Treatment:
- Daily participation in structured residential programming (at least 5 days per week, 3+ hours per day)
- Trauma-informed substance use counseling
- Education about how alcohol affects the brain
- Identifying triggers related to military trauma and loneliness
- Weekly monitoring of craving levels
- Random alcohol and drug testing
- Relapse prevention planning

Mental Health Treatment:
- Psychiatric medication management (recommended medications include Vistaril for anxiety, Sertraline for mood, Naltrexone for cravings, Prazosin for PTSD, and Trazodone for insomnia - client initially refused)
- Weekly individual therapy using CBT and motivational interviewing
- Weekly trauma-focused therapy
- Grounding techniques and emotional regulation training
- Sleep hygiene education
- Mindfulness-based stress reduction exercises
- Ongoing suicide risk monitoring (currently low risk)

Case Management:
- Intensive case management
- Referral to VA services for PTSD support and housing assistance
- Vocational readiness assessment
- Community resource connections
- Help building a sober support network before discharge

Support Services:
- At least 3 peer-support meetings per week (AA or Veteran Recovery Group)
- Weekly meetings with case manager for housing applications
- Exploration of VA benefits within 30 days
crisisInterventionPlan:
Triggers:
- Gun fire
- "Anything with a bum"
- Stress and emotional distress
- Loneliness and isolation
- Being in environments where alcohol is present

Warning Signs:
- Increased alcohol cravings
- Worsening depression or anxiety
- Trouble sleeping
- Intrusive thoughts about drinking or trauma
- Isolating from others
- Mood changes

Intervention Strategies:
- Contact treatment staff immediately
- Use grounding techniques and emotional regulation skills
- Call crisis hotline
- Attend extra peer support meetings
- Use safety plan
- Reach out to case manager or sponsor
- Review relapse prevention plan
- Practice coping skills learned in therapy

- Crisis hotline information will be provided
- Emergency contacts will be documented
- Safety plan will be reviewed and signed
